All prices as of 2034 GMT

EQUITIES

GLOBAL - Blockbuster results from chipmaker TSMC bounced Wall Street higher after two days of losses on Tuesday, while oil and gold fell off recent highs as demand for safe-haven assets subsided on signals U.S. President Donald Trump might be less inclined to take immediate military action against Iran.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

NEW YORK - U.S. stocks rose on Thursday after two days of declines as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs shares shot up following upbeat quarterly results, while technology shares climbed after chipmaker TSMC's blockbuster results.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 378.05 points, or 0.76%, to 49,523.22, the S&P 500 gained 33.55 points, or 0.47%, to 6,959.15 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 132.78 points, or 0.55%, to 23,600.10.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

LONDON - European shares scaled a new record high on Thursday, boosted by technology and financial stocks as investors assessed several positive earnings updates and signs of resilience in the German economy.

The pan-European STOXX 600 advanced 0.5% to 614.57 points.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TOKYO - Japan's Nikkei share average fell back from a record high on Thursday, as the yen's rebound weighed on exporters and a red-hot rally in technology shares lost steam.

The benchmark Nikkei 225 Index fell 0.4% to close at 54,110.50, snapping a three-day rise that took the gauge to an all-time high on Wednesday.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SHANGHAI - Chinese stocks extended losses on Thursday, pausing a blistering start to the year, after regulators moved to rein in speculative trading.

The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index closed down 0.3% at 4,112.60, marking its third consecutive session of losses and pulling further back. The blue-chip CSI 300 Index edged up 0.2% after swinging between gains and losses throughout the day.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

AUSTRALIA -Australian shares were poised to open lower on Friday, with index heavyweight miners likely to fall on lower iron ore and copper prices after contributing the bulk of the index's recent gains.

The local share price index futures (YAPcm1) fell 0.03%, a 30.7-point discount to the underlying S&P/ASX 200 index close. The benchmark rose 0.5% on Thursday.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SEOUL - The benchmark KOSPI closed up 74.45 points, or 1.58%, at 4,797.55, extending its gaining streak to a 10th straight session and marking the highest closing level on record.

The KOSPI rose for the 10th straight session on expectations of a recovery in domestic demand, despite a hawkish central bank meeting," said Lee Kyoung-min, an analyst at Daishin Securities.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

FOREIGN EXCHANGE

NEW YORK - The dollar reached a six-week high on Thursday after data showed that the number of Americans filing new applications for unemployment benefits unexpectedly fell last week, further boosting expectations that the Federal Reserve will keep rates on hold for the next several months.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, rose 0.24% to 99.31, and reached 99.49, the highest since December 2.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

CHINA - China's yuan climbed to a fresh 32-month high against the dollar on Thursday, underpinned by a stronger midpoint guidance fix, though it fell short of market expectations.

In the spot market, the onshore yuan at one point strengthened to a high of 6.9678 per dollar, the strongest level since May 2023, before changing hands at 6.9702 as of 0400 GMT.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

AUSTRALIA - The Australian and New Zealand dollars struggled on Thursday as a sell-off in global equities and a bounce in the Japanese yen amid heightened geopolitical risks left both currencies pinned near key support levels.

The Aussie was flat at $0.6682 , after moving little overnight

For a full report, click on

- - - -

SEOUL - The Korean won weakened against dollar on Thursday.

The won was quoted at 1,472.2 per dollar on the onshore settlement platform , 0.56% lower than its previous close at 1,464.0.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TREASURIES

NEW YORK - U.S. Treasury yields were mostly higher on Thursday, after a round of economic data came in stronger than expected, slightly denting expectations the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates in the near term.

The yield on the benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury note (US10YT=TWEB) gained 1.6 basis points to 4.156%.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

LONDON - Euro zone bond yields held steady in afternoon trading on Thursday after hitting an over-one-month low the day before when a small bout of risk aversion gripped markets and spurred investors to seek safety.

Germany's 10-year yield, the benchmark for the euro zone, was flat at 2.82%, while its two-year yield was up by one basis point at 2.09%. (DE10YT=RRDE2YT=RR)

For a full report, click on

- - - -

TOKYO - Japanese government bonds (JGBs) rallied on Thursday as the yen rebounded, recovering after a sharp selloff on expectations of a snap election.

The benchmark 10-year JGB yield (JP10YTN=JBTC) fell 2.5 basis points (bps) to 2.155%, retreating from 2.185%, its highest level since February 1999, reached on Wednesday.

For a full report, click on

COMMODITIES

GOLD - Gold prices declined on Thursday as weaker-than-expected U.S. weekly jobless claims data boosted the dollar, while U.S. President Donald Trump's moderated tone on Iran further weighed on safe-haven demand for the metal.

Spot gold was down 0.1% at $4,614.93 per ounce, as of 01:30 p.m. ET (1830 GMT).

For a full report, click on

- - - -

IRON ORE

Iron ore futures retreated on Thursday as hot metal output fell in top buyer China amid a slow resumption of production after the New Year holiday.

The most-traded May iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) closed daytime trade 1.03% lower at 813 yuan ($116.63) a metric ton.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

BASE METALS

Copper pulled back from a record high on Thursday on a stronger dollar and easing concerns over the potential imposition of U.S. tariffs on the metal.

Benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange was down 0.6% at $13,108 a metric ton as of 1702 GMT, having touched a record $13,407 on Wednesday.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

OIL

Oil prices slid more than $3 and were poised to end a five-day streak of gains on Thursday after U.S. President Donald Trump said the crackdown on protesters in Iran was easing, allaying concerns over potential military action against Iran and oil supply disruptions.

Brent futures were down $3.11 or 4.68%, at $63.41 a barrel by 12:13 p.m. EDT (1713 GMT).

For a full report, click on

- - - -

PALM OIL

Malaysian palm oil futures fell more than 1% lower on Thursday for a third straight session, weighed down by weaker rival edible oils, crude oil prices, and Indonesia's plan to scrap its B50 biodiesel plans.

The benchmark palm oil contract for March delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange slid 60 ringgit, or 1.49%, to 3,980 ringgit ($982.23) a metric ton at the close.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

RUBBER

Japanese rubber futures slid on Thursday from a 10-month peak hit in the previous session, as profit-booking ahead of the weekend and a decline in oil prices weighed on prices.

The Osaka Exchange (OSE) rubber contract for June delivery , was down 2.8 yen, or 0.78%, at 354.1 yen ($2.23) per kg.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

data.title: Paramount drops after Delaware judge refuses to speed up Warner Bros lawsuit
data.description:
** Shares of Paramount Skydance down 2.27% at $11.78 after a Delaware judge refused to speed up its lawsuit against Warner Bros Discovery

** PSKY is on track for three straight sessions of losses and lowest close since August last year

** PSKY is demanding that WBD better explain its decision that Netflix's proposed $82.7 billion takeover was better than Paramount's own $108.7 billion hostile bid

** The lawsuit was part of David Ellison-led Paramount's effort to ratchet up pressure on Warner Bros

** Paramount's $30/share all-cash tender offer expires on January 21

** Including session moves, PSKY down 12% YTD

data.title: IMF's Georgieva says latest growth forecasts to show resilience to global trade shocks
data.description:
The International Monetary Fund's latest economic forecasts due next week will show the global economy's continued resilience to trade shocks and "fairly strong" growth, IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva told Reuters on Thursday.

In an interview during a visit to Kyiv to discuss the IMF's loan to Ukraine, Georgieva would not say specifically whether the IMF would again revise its forecasts slightly upward as the World Bank did this week.

In October, the IMF revised upward its 2025 global GDP growth forecast slightly to 3.2% from 3.0% in July as the drag from U.S. tariffs was less than initially feared. It kept its 2026 global growth outlook unchanged at 3.1%.

Asked what the January forecasts would show, Georgieva said: "More of the same - that the world economy is remarkably resilient, that trade shock has not derailed global growth, that risks are more tilted to the downside, even if performance now is fairly strong."

The IMF is expected to release its World Economic Outlook update on January 19.

data.title: Influencer Who Had Child With Elon Musk Sues xAI Over Deepfake Images — WSJ
data.description:
By Georgia Wells

Ashley St. Clair sued Elon Musk's artificial intelligence company on Thursday, alleging that its Grok chatbot is "unreasonably dangerous as designed" and constitutes a public nuisance.

She also is seeking a temporary restraining order to stop xAI's Grok from creating images that undress her.

St. Clair, a conservative influencer who had a child with Musk, previously said that users on X were using Grok to undress images of her and other women and children.

"She lives in fear that nude and sexual images of herself, including of her as a child, will continue to be created by xAI and that she will not be safe from the people who consume these images," one of her filings said.

St. Clair's lawyer, Carrie Goldberg, with the firm C.A. Goldberg, filed the case in New York county, where St. Clair lives. It has been moved to federal court.

xAI launched a suit against St. Clair in Texas on Thursday, claiming she has breached the company's terms of service agreement with her suit.

xAI didn't immediately respond to a message seeking comment. A lawyer for xAI said in the company's filing that when St. Clair signed up for an account, she agreed to the company's terms of service, which require disputes to be brought to courts in Texas.

Write to Georgia Wells at georgia.wells@wsj.com

data.title: Virgina County to Sell $376.8 Million of Bonds for Public Works
data.description:
By Paulo Trevisani

Fairfax County, Va., plans to issue $376.8 million of bonds to finance public works projects.

The tax-exempt Public Improvement Bonds Series 2026 A will mature between 2026 and 2045, according to a document posted Wednesday on MuniOS.

The securities will be sold in a competitive sale. Offers can be submitted electronically through the BiDCOMP/Parity Competitive Bidding System until 10:30 a.m. ET on Jan. 21, according to the notice of sale.

The securities are backed by revenue collected from property taxes. For the fiscal year ended June 30, Fairfax's total general fund tax revenues were reported at $4.8 billion.

Proceeds will be used to finance improvements for local schools and transportation, along with public safety, parks and library facilities.

The bonds are rated AAA by both S&P Global Ratings and Fitch Ratings, and Aaa by Moody's.

Write to Paulo Trevisani at paulo.trevisani@wsj.com

data.title: CBOT corn ends lower on ample world grain supplies
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade corn futures ended lower on Thursday, pressured by inter-market spreading against soybeans and reminders of plentiful U.S. and world grain supplies, analysts said.

  • CBOT March corn (CH26) settled down 1-3/4 cents at $4.20-1/4 per bushel.

  • The International Grains Council raised its estimate of world corn production for the current 2025/26 season to 1.313 billion metric tons, up 15 million tons from its previous monthly estimate.

  • Traders continue to digest Monday's larger-than-expected USDA estimates of U.S. 2025 corn production and December 1 quarterly stocks.

  • Brazilian government agency Conab estimated the country's total 2025/26 corn production at 138.87 million metric tons, nearly unchanged from its month-ago view of 138.88 million.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. corn in the week to January 8 at 1,139,500 metric tons, in line with trade expectations for 600,000 to 1,400,000 tons.

  • Separately, under its daily reporting rules, the USDA confirmed private sales of 500,302 metric tons of corn to undisclosed destinations and 260,000 tons to Japan.

data.title: Hogs Surge After Goldman Roll Concludes — Market Talk
data.description: The lean hog futures contract jumps 2.5% to 87.925 cents a pound. The session was the first to occur after the "Goldman roll" — a five-day period each month where the Goldman Sachs Commodity Index is rebalanced. Hog futures have seen increased activity during this period, with this week seeing more volume of hog futures traded than the prior week. Last Friday's CFTC Commitment of Traders report says that fund traders have added long hog positions. However, slaughtered hogs are growing heavier, meaning that more meat is making it into the supply pipeline. Live cattle close up 0.4% to $2.362 a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Graham Holdings Raises Dividend by 4.4%
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Graham Holdings raised its quarterly dividend by 4.4%, to $1.88 a share from $1.80.

The new payout, equal to $7.52 a year, represents an annual yield of about 0.7% based on Wednesday's closing price of $1149.89.

The dividend is payable Feb. 19 to stockholders of record Feb. 4, the holding company said.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Treasury Yields Rise on Robust Economic Indicators — Market Talk
data.description:
1538 ET - Treasury yields recover from two consecutive declining days after better-than-expected U.S. economic indicators. Jobless claims surprisingly fall, signaling that layoffs remain contained. Regional Fed surveys in Philadelphia and New York show manufacturing activity hotter than expected. Tomorrow, December industrial production growth is forecast to slow to 0.1% from 0.2%, in a WSJ consensus. The 10-year yield adds 0.020 percentage point to 4.159% and the two-year rises 0.050 p.p. to 3.563%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1520 ET - Taiwan Semiconductor's earnings release largely beat expectations and shows that the company is "firing on all cylinders," say Wedbush analysts Matt Bryson and Antoine Legault. Fourth-quarter gross margin was a pleasant surprise, while the forecast for 30% growth in dollar terms in 2026 at least matched heightened expectations. "Any competitive risk seemingly still years away (arguably the end of this decade at the earliest), and with the stock trading at a 30% discount to our PT (which could be construed as overly conservative with TSMC projecting a LT growth rate well above our multiple), the company remains one of our favorite names in hardware," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1449 ET - A big question facing markets this year is to what extent data centers are "crowding out" investments in other sectors, says Carlyle's Jason Thomas in a note. Investment imbalances in sectors such as real estate and infrastructure challenge the assumption that AI is boosting what otherwise would be a stagnant economy, Thomas says. "Since the introduction of ChatGPT in November 2022, data center assets put-in-place have grown 3.2x; all other real estate development is up less than 10%," he says. "The same trends are evident in infrastructure," where data center deals have grown to roughly two-thirds of the entire market from less than a quarter of closed transaction volumes since 2020, he adds. Instead of looking only at the risks related to AI itself, "it may be time to consider economy-wide resource constraints." (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1327 ET - Airbnb's AI prospects are improving, Mizuho analysts say. The short-term rental company just hired a new chief technology officer, which the analysts say gives them some confidence about Airbnb's AI product timeline. AI could also be a potential path for Airbnb to integrate more hotel supply into its platform, the analysts say. Over the course of 2026, the analysts expect Airbnb to improve its hotel product and expand beyond its first three focus markets of New York, Los Angeles and Madrid. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1305 ET - Asking rents declined for the 29th consecutive month year-over-year in December, Realtor.com says, but the latest data show the easing is increasingly concentrated among higher-priced rentals, leaving many lower-cost renters with little relief. The median asking rent across the 50 largest U.S. metros was $1,689, down 0.7% from December 2024. While the national median rent has softened, the report finds that lower-priced rentals have seen the most rent growth since before the pandemic. December's decline was the smallest annual drop since March, reflecting a pattern of rent declines flattening out at the end of 2025 after larger drops during the summer months. Across unit sizes, studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom asking rents all declined again year-over-year, though studios are closer to flat than one- and two-bedroom units. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1256 ET - U.S. bank results bode well for European peers and showed encouraging trends in trading, investment banking and wealth management, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analysts say in a note. Among European banks, UBS looks best positioned to gain from these trends, the analysts say. "Overall, despite the mixed share-price reactions, we believe the U.S. results reinforce our more constructive stance on the EU banks sub-sector, with [Barclays] remaining our top pick," the analysts say. The Stoxx Europe 600 Banks index closes 0.6% higher. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1235 ET - A mortgage payment on a typical home is forecast to be affordable in 20 major metropolitan areas by the end of the year, Zillow says. Zillow expects slow but steady home value growth, falling mortgage rates and rising incomes to contribute to a nationwide improvement in affordability this year. Affordability in this case means a mortgage payment on a typical house that doesn't require more than 30% of the median household income. When housing costs rise above that 30% threshold, they become a financial burden. Prices soared starting in 2020, and affordability declined sharply in 2022, when mortgage rates doubled. Affordability reached all-time lows in October 2023, when a typical mortgage required 38.2% of median household income. At the national level, a mortgage payment now takes 32.6% of median household income, already the best affordability seen nationwide since August 2022.(chris.wack@wsj.com)

1208 ET - Consumer lending was strong in the fourth quarter, BMO Capital Markets analysts say in a research note, and that strength likely benefited credit bureaus and scoring companies such as Equifax, Fair Isaac and TransUnion. The analysts caution, however, that momentum may not carry cleanly into the coming year. "Our main concern on the 2026 guidance is lender affordability and the extent to which this could meaningfully reduce industry volumes as lenders pull less credit," they write. As a result, they are keeping estimates for the year conservative, rather than flowing through upside from the recent quarter, despite bullish commentary on the lending environment. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1141 ET — Under Armour announced three executive-leadership changes focused on marketing and product as the latest move in the retailer's turnaround strategy. Kara Trent was named chief merchandising officer, where she will manage the product line, assortment and channel segmentation. Yassine Saidi, who has been serving as chief product officer, will move into a senior adviser role focused on design and creative continuity. The goal is to drive better category performance and align with consumer demand. Under Armour's turnaround strategy includes cutting out some products to focus on the company's core items, as well as restructuring operations to save costs. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1141 ET - Rapid changes in the geopolitical situation make 2026 tricky for oil markets as they flip between fundamental concerns and shifting risk premium, Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities says in a note. "Notional ideas rooted in fundamentals are going to frequently take a back seat in favor of the ever-increasing pace of change in global geopolitics," he says. "While prices are in decline today on some relaxation in U.S. rhetoric, the next leg up may be in a matter of days." Crude futures are down sharply on reduced expectations of U.S. strikes on Iran. WTI is off 4.5% at $59.17 a barrel, and Brent is down 4.5% at $63.54. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1136 ET - BlackRock ended the year with more than $14 trillion in assets, a record for the world's largest money manager. The firm has benefited from buoyant markets, as an extended rally has fueled corporate and investor confidence, lifting the profits at banks, brokers and money managers. Investors have also largely cheered CEO Larry Fink's strategy to transform BlackRock into a do-everything asset manager that manages private assets for clients along with its bread-and-butter public stocks and bonds. BlackRock's quarterly profit fell in the recent quarter, hurt by one-time costs stemming from recent acquisitions, though adjusted earnings and the net inflow of money from clients topped analyst estimates. Shares climb 5%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1124 ET - Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon expects an uptick in IPOs over the next few years, so long as the market environment remains as constructive as it is now. "We've got a lot of big companies in the pipeline that I think, just for a variety of reasons, are reaching a moment in time where they're saying, 'You know what? It's time to go,'" he says on a call with analysts. If these companies do in fact go public, Solomon says it would be constructive for the overall market. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Taiwan Pledges $250 Billion in U.S. Spending in Exchange for Lower Tariffs — WSJ
data.description:
By Amrith Ramkumar

The U.S. and Taiwan signed a trade deal Thursday aimed at boosting American production of semiconductors in exchange for lower tariffs, adding to the Trump administration's efforts to bring critical industries to the U.S.

Under the agreement, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing will add several new factories to its cluster in Arizona as part of a $250 billion investment in the U.S., the Commerce Department said. In exchange, the U.S. is cutting tariffs on Taiwanese goods to 15%, from 20%, and exempting Taiwanese chip companies like TSMC that are investing more in America. The contours of the deal were reported earlier in the week.

The agreement adds to deals with trading partners including the European Union and Japan and a truce with China. The Trump administration is attempting to de-escalate tensions over tariffs to avoid denting the economy while securing added investment in the U.S.

The Taiwan deal focuses on semiconductors because of the dominance of TSMC, which essentially has a monopoly on making the advanced chips that power the modern economy. They are critical for customers from Nvidia to Apple that want to build data centers for training AI models or sell consumer products.

TSMC has received several billion dollars in subsidies through the 2022 Chips and Science Act and will have roughly a dozen plants in Arizona after its latest expansion, including some advanced manufacturing capabilities that give it a sizable footprint outside Taiwan.

Write to Amrith Ramkumar at amrith.ramkumar@wsj.com

data.title: Cathie Wood Is Selling Tesla. Here's the Chip Stock She's Buying. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Cathie Wood, the architect behind the ARK Invest funds, is famously known as a Tesla bull. However, her funds have been selling the stock while doubling down on shares of a semiconductor titan that was recently added to the ARK portfolio.

A Wednesday trading disclosure shows the ARK Next Generation Internet exchange-traded fund bought more Broadcom stock after buying into the semiconductor company last week. The fund increased its stake by 31,308 shares, bringing its holdings to 62,545 shares as of Thursday.

The investment management company also added Broadcom stock to a different fund. The ARK Innovation ETF picked up 111,781 shares, representing a new stake in the chip maker.

ARK was buying at a low. Shares slipped 4.2% on Wednesday amid a broad sell-off in technology stocks, a drop that was compounded by a report that Beijing had banned software from a dozen U.S. and Israeli companies including Broadcom. The stock quickly rebounded and was up 2% at $346.73 on Thursday.

Separately, the ARK Innovation fund shed 86,139 shares of Tesla, its largest holding by weight. While Wood's funds have actively been selling the stock, this shouldn't be taken as a sign they're backing out entirely — Tesla remains the largest holding in several ARK funds. ARK Innovation held 1,728,504 Tesla shares as of Thursday, representing nearly 10% of the portfolio.

Tesla stock has wavered so far into 2026. On Wednesday, CEO Elon Musk announced the company would start offering its full self-driving software only as a monthly subscription, sending shares down 1.8%.

Other notable changes to ARK's funds included the sale of 19,310 shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing ahead of the chip maker's earnings report on Thursday.

The ARK Next Generation Internet ETF held 164,437 U.S.-listed shares following the transaction, which were worth around $53.8 million. As of Wednesday, the stake was the 13th-largest component of the fund's portfolio.

Taiwan Semiconductor posted fourth-quarter earnings that handily topped expectations and issued a forecast that pointed to heavy spending in 2026. The outlook reignited hopes for a lasting artificial-intelligence boom and lifted stocks across the sector. Shares in Taiwan Semi itself were up 5.4% on Thursday.

Another fund, the ARK Fintech Innovation ETF, snapped up 56,993 shares in Klarna Group, the buy-now, pay-later service provider that went public late last year. The ETF delivered a 29% return in 2025, outstripping a 20% gain for the Nasdaq Composite.

Inside Scoop is a regular Barron's feature covering stock transactions by corporate executives and board members — so-called insiders — as well as large shareholders, politicians, and other prominent figures. Due to their insider status, these investors are required to disclose stock trades with the Securities and Exchange Commission or other regulatory groups.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Treasuries Give Back Ground As Jobless Claims Unexpectedly Dip
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Treasuries moved to the downside during trading on Thursday, giving back ground after moving higher over the two previous sessions.

Bond prices fluctuated early in the session but spent most of the day in negative territory. As a result, the yield on the benchmark ten-year note, which moves opposite of its price, rose 2.0 basis points to 4.160 percent.

The pullback by treasuries came as the Labor Department released a report showing first-time claims for U.S. unemployment benefits unexpectedly dipped in the week ended January 10th, partly offsetting concerns about the strength of the labor market.

The Labor Department said initial jobless claims fell to 198,000, a decrease of 9,000 from the previous week's revised level of 207,000.

Economists had expected jobless claims to rise to 215,000 from the 208,000 originally reported for the previous week.

'Initial jobless claims are still subject to seasonal volatility, but the surprises have been more to the downside,' said Nancy Vanden Houten, Lead U.S. Economist at Oxford Economics. 'Looking through the noise, we see no signs that labor market conditions are worsening.'

Upbeat reports on regional manufacturing activity in Philadelphia and New York may have also reduced treasuries' safe haven appeal.

Easing concerns about a confrontation between the U.S. and Iran may also have weighed on treasuries as President Donald Trump seemed to suggest the U.S. would not attack the country over crackdowns on protestors.

Looking ahead, trading on Friday may be impacted by reaction to reports on industrial production and homebuilder confidence.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Phoenix Osteopathy College To Sell Bonds For Facility Development
data.description:
By Patrick Sheridan

The Industrial Development Authority of the City of Phoenix, Arizona plans to sell $89.6 million in bonds to finance or refinance a portion of the costs of developing a campus facility for The Valley College of Osteopathic Medicine.

The Series 2026 Taxable Educational Facilities Revenue Bonds have a due date in 2055, and are slated to be priced as early as the week of Jan. 26, with settlement scheduled as early as the week of March 2 according to the roadshow document accompanying the preliminary limited offering memorandum posted Wednesday on MuniOS.

Interest on the bonds is payable on June 1, 2026, and on each December and June thereafter.

The bonds are secured by a loan agreement in which The Valley College of Osteopathic Medicine.

Bond proceeds will be used to develop a campus facility in Phoenix, for the operation of college of osteopathic medicine. Funds will also be used to pay off debt used to acquire the building, originally constructed in 2010 for Blue Cross Blue Shield of Arizona, that will house the future school. The facility will be equipped to accept the initial class of students by the summer of 2026, according to the preliminary limited offering memorandum.

According to its website, in 2030, when the proposed school is fully operational, it will have an positive economic impact in the area of $58.5 million, support more than 148 jobs, and generate $1.8 million in taxes for communities in the region.

The College has applied to Moody's Ratings for a bond rating.

Morgan Stanley is the underwriter of the offering.

Write to Patrick Sheridan at patrick.sheridan@wsj.com
